h2h_summary: Qatar and Switzerland have met only once before, with Qatar winning 1-0 in a November 2018 friendly via a late Akram Afif goal.
team_style_home: Qatar under Julen Lopetegui set up in a compact 4-2-3-1, prioritising defensive solidity, counter-attacking football, and set-piece threats, with Akram Afif as the primary creative outlet in the half-spaces.
team_style_away: Switzerland under Murat Yakin deploy a flexible 3-4-3 or 4-3-3, pressing aggressively man-to-man, building patiently from the back through the Xhaka-Freuler double pivot, with Ndoye and Vargas providing width.
wc_history_home: Qatar made history as the 2022 hosts but became the first host nation to lose all three group stage games, exiting without a point in a 7-1 aggregate defeat; the 2026 tournament marks their first World Cup qualification via the standard AFC process.
wc_history_away: Switzerland are competing at their sixth consecutive World Cup and have reached the quarter-finals on three occasions (1934, 1938, 1954), most recently advancing to the last 16 in 2022 before losing to Portugal.
group_context: Group B contains co-hosts Canada, Switzerland, Qatar, and Bosnia and Herzegovina; Switzerland and Canada are the favourites to advance, making Qatar vs Switzerland a crucial early match where a Qatar defeat would likely end their realistic hopes of progression.
strengths_home: Akram Afif's creativity and ability to unlock defences on the counter
Asian Cup pedigree and experience of playing in major tournaments
Defensive discipline and set-piece threat under Lopetegui's structured system
strengths_away: Experienced, cohesive squad featuring Xhaka in his fourth World Cup
High-pressing system that disrupts opponents and generates turnovers
Strong recent form including a 4-1 win over Jordan and solid qualifying campaign
